Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

W. V. O. Quine

1908 – 2000

Naturalized epistemology, behavior, and a deflationary treatment of the mental.

The most accurate starting point is this: Quine did not produce a single, self-contained “philosophy of mind” book, and he did not treat the soul as a traditional theological or metaphysical topic in the way a scholastic, Cartesian, or phenomenologist would. But his work contains enough on mind, language, ontology, naturalism, behavior, intentionality, and consciousness to reconstruct a serious and coherent philosophy of mind. That philosophy is austere, naturalistic, anti-Cartesian, physicalist, and publicly disciplined, but it is not simply crude behaviorism and not simply eliminativism. It is best understood as a physicalist philosophy of embodied minds, combined with a deep suspicion of private mental objects, meanings, propositions, and souls as entities in one’s official ontology.

The theological dimension is not incidental. Quine’s treatment of “Homer’s gods,” his criterion of ontological commitment, his occasional remarks about disembodied spirits, and his naturalistic refusal of an extra-scientific standpoint all bear directly on theological questions about soul, spirit, God, immortality, and the metaphysical status of consciousness. Quine’s framework does not merely say “theology is false”; rather, it asks whether theological entities earn their keep in the same disciplined ontology as electrons, bodies, sets, and other theoretical posits. That is a more subtle, and in some ways more challenging, position.

1. The relevant Quinean corpus

The central texts for extracting Quine’s philosophy of mind include “On Mental Entities,” “Word and Object,” “The Scope and Language of Science,” “Epistemology Naturalized,” “Mind and Verbal Dispositions,” “States of Mind,” “Mind, Brain, and Behavior,” and the late German lecture “Die Entwicklung von Bewusstsein und Sprache,” later published in English as “The Growth of Mind and Language.” Quine’s official bibliography confirms the importance of “States of Mind” and “Mind, Brain, and Behavior,” while also recording his late German-language paper on consciousness and language.

This distribution matters. Quine’s philosophy of mind is not a freestanding theory of qualia, selfhood, or soul. It is an outgrowth of his wider commitments: naturalism, holism, extensionalism, behaviorally constrained semantics, ontological austerity, and the demand for regimented theory. The mind appears in Quine not as a privileged metaphysical region but as one difficult sector within the same natural world that includes bodies, stimuli, language learning, social behavior, and scientific theory.

The reconstructed position can be summarized this way: minds are not immaterial substances; mental events are events or states of bodies; consciousness is not denied but is treated as a natural phenomenon whose scientific articulation remains difficult; intentional idioms such as belief and desire are indispensable in ordinary and social-scientific life but do not belong unchanged in an austere canonical language; and talk of souls or spirits has no Quinean ontological standing unless it can be integrated into our best overall theory of the world.

2. Naturalism: no “first philosophy” of mind

Quine’s philosophy of mind begins with his naturalism. There is no standpoint outside science from which philosophy first certifies science, mind, body, meaning, or reality. Philosophy is continuous with science, and “science” here is broad: it includes psychology, history, sociology, economics, and ordinary empirical inquiry, not merely physics. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes Quine’s naturalism as the view that reality is identified and described from within science, with no distinctively philosophical standpoint prior to or above it.

This is decisive for mind. A Cartesian philosophy of mind begins from the first-person certainty of consciousness and then asks how an external world or other minds can be known. Quine reverses the order. He begins from within our inherited scientific/common-sense picture of organisms in a physical world. The human subject is already an embodied natural creature. Epistemology itself becomes a study of how sensory stimulation leads, through psychological and linguistic processes, to theories of the world. Quine’s naturalized epistemology studies the physical human subject as receiving sensory input and producing world-describing output.

This does not mean that Quine thinks psychology is reducible in practice to physics. It means that psychology, language theory, and philosophy of mind are constrained by the same empirical and theoretical virtues as the rest of science. Minds are to be studied as parts of nature, not as windows into a supernatural or pre-natural domain.

So Quine’s first move against the soul is methodological rather than dogmatic. He does not begin by proving that an immaterial soul is impossible. He begins by denying that the philosopher has a special, pre-scientific access to such a soul. If the soul is to be admitted into ontology, it must be admitted as a posit within our best overall theory. It cannot be smuggled in as the hidden owner of experience merely because experience is introspectively vivid.

3. Ontological commitment: why mental entities are suspect

Quine’s most famous ontological question is: what does our best theory say there is? His mature view ties ontology to the bound variables of a regimented theory: what a theory quantifies over, when paraphrased into canonical form, is what that theory is committed to. In Quine’s mature ontology, the main admitted entities are physical objects and sets; he excludes, or at least resists, properties, propositions, possible entities, and meanings because their identity conditions are obscure.

This matters for mind because many traditional philosophies of mind populate the world with mental objects: ideas, sense-data, meanings, propositions, qualia, acts of consciousness, Cartesian egos, souls, and perhaps divine or angelic minds. Quine’s pressure is always the same: what are the identity conditions of these entities? How do they improve theory? Can we paraphrase away apparent reference to them? Are we merely projecting grammatical form into ontology?

This does not entail that Quine denies pain, perception, consciousness, thought, or belief in ordinary language. His point is more exacting. The question is whether we must posit mental entities, as distinct from physical organisms and their states, in the official inventory of what exists. His answer is generally no. Mental vocabulary may remain useful, but that does not automatically establish a special class of mental objects.

Quine’s physicalism is explicit. The SEP summarizes his view with the thought that even the smallest mental occurrence must involve some physical difference. In Quinean terms: if there is a real mental event, then it is not an event floating free of physical reality. It is a state or episode of an organism, ultimately of a physical system.

Here one must be careful. Quine’s ontology is not “only particles,” and his scientific naturalism is not a claim that all good explanation must be neurophysiology. His ontology includes physical objects broadly construed, including organisms, and it also includes sets because mathematics is indispensable to science. But he does not allow a separate mental substance merely because grammar tempts us to reify “thoughts,” “ideas,” or “experiences.”

4. Mind-body: physical monism, not Cartesian dualism

Quine’s mind-body position is physicalist monism. In “The Scope and Language of Science,” he argues that a physical ontology can accommodate states of mind by identifying mental episodes with temporal parts or states of the physical organism. Hallucinating, being inspired, or feeling pain need not require mental objects over and above the organism; they can be treated as bodily states, even if our predicates for them are vague and not easily translated into neurophysiology.

His argument against dualism is partly scientific and partly ontological. Substance dualism faces the classic interaction problem: how does an immaterial mind causally affect a physical body without violating the closure of physical explanation? Quine’s response is not merely that dualism is false but that it becomes redundant if every mental occurrence relevant to behavior is associated with a physical state. Unless one posits genuinely disembodied spirits, dualism collapses into physical monism.

That last qualification is important for theology. Quine recognizes that disembodied spirits are the point at which dualism would not simply reduce to bodily states. But he does not endorse them. He treats them as the kind of extra posit that would need independent theoretical justification. If all the evidence for someone’s thoughts, pains, beliefs, and perceptions lies in bodily behavior, speech, physiology, and neural organization, then positing an additional soul-substance does no explanatory work by Quine’s standards.

So the Quinean position on the soul is not: “the word soul is meaningless.” Nor is it: “science has disproved the soul in a single experiment.” It is: “the soul, as an entity distinct from the body, is not demanded by our best regimented theory; mental life can be accounted for by organisms, dispositions, neural states, and public behavior; therefore the soul is not part of our canonical ontology.”

One could still use “soul” poetically, morally, religiously, or anthropologically to mean the whole person, the depth of character, the living center of agency, or the integrated pattern of dispositions that constitutes a human life. But that would not be a Cartesian or scholastic soul in Quine’s official ontology. It would be a way of speaking about embodied persons.

5. Consciousness: not denied, but deprivileged

A common misreading is that Quine “denies consciousness” because he rejects mental entities. That is too crude. In “On Mental Entities,” Quine explicitly distinguishes repudiating mental entities from denying that we sense or are conscious. The issue is not whether consciousness occurs, but whether the facts require irreducibly mental entities in addition to physical states and processes.

His attitude toward consciousness is austere but not eliminative. Pain can be construed as a physical state of the organism. Consciousness, if understood as a capacity to respond to one’s own responses, can be construed as a state of a physical object. The problem is not whether consciousness is real; the problem is whether a theory improves by positing a separate mental realm.

Portuguese-language Quine scholarship is helpful here because it emphasizes an often-overlooked nuance: Quine’s naturalized philosophy of mind can look as though it excludes consciousness, but Quine’s better answer is that consciousness is a mystery to be investigated, not a datum to be thrown away. On this reading, we know what it is to be conscious, but we do not yet know how to express that state scientifically; whatever consciousness is, it is a bodily or nervous phenomenon.

This gives us a Quinean treatment of the so-called “hard problem” of consciousness, though Quine did not formulate it in Chalmers’s later terms. He would likely resist treating “raw feels” as private objects with special ontological status. He would ask instead: what is the scientific and behavioral role of our consciousness vocabulary? How are consciousness terms learned? What public symptoms anchor them? What bodily states do they track? What explanatory work do they perform?

Quine’s view is therefore not phenomenologically rich. He does not provide a deep description of lived temporality, embodiment, affect, or subjectivity. But he also does not need to say that consciousness is unreal. His position is: consciousness is real insofar as conscious organisms are real and conscious episodes are states of such organisms; what is suspect is the conversion of consciousness into a private object, substance, or epistemic foundation.

6. Language, publicity, and the rejection of private meanings

Quine’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his philosophy of language. “Language is a social art,” he says in “Word and Object”; language learning depends on publicly available cues, not on access to private mental meanings.

This thesis is one of the main engines of his anti-Cartesianism. If language is learned through public stimuli, correction, assent, dissent, and shared behavioral circumstances, then meanings are not private inner objects that each speaker consults. Quine’s famous attack on the “museum myth” of meaning rejects the idea that words are labels attached to private exhibits in the mind. Spanish-language discussions of Quine’s epistemology stress precisely this point: his empiricist-naturalist account of language learning opposes the reification of mental entities as meanings.

The same principle applies to mental vocabulary. We learn terms for pain, joy, anxiety, perception, and awareness through public criteria: behavior, expression, circumstances, verbal training, and social interaction. This does not mean that pain is nothing but pain behavior. It means that the public anchoring of mental language prevents private mental objects from becoming the foundation of semantics.

Sander Verhaegh’s work on Quine is especially useful here. He emphasizes that Quine’s mental concepts are socially and publicly learned: terms for anxieties, aches, joys, and awareness are acquired through observable symptoms. But Verhaegh also argues that it is a mistake to treat Quine as a simple logical behaviorist, because Quine rejects the idea that each mental statement must be translatable into a behavioral statement.

That distinction is central. Quine is a behavioral methodologist, not a behaviorist reductionist. The evidence for language and mind must be public, but the theoretical terms we use to systematize that evidence may go beyond immediately observable behavior. This is exactly how science works elsewhere: we infer hidden structures, dispositions, and mechanisms from observable effects.

7. Dispositions: the hinge between behavior and body

Quine’s favorite mediating notion is disposition. A disposition is not merely an observed behavior. Solubility is not the same as actually dissolving; fragility is not the same as actually breaking; irritability, belief, or verbal assent-disposition is not the same as a single overt action. A disposition is a real physical trait, structure, or mechanism manifested under certain conditions.

In “Word and Object,” Quine connects meaning, assent, and language learning to dispositions to verbal behavior. A person’s readiness to assent to a sentence under certain stimulations is not an occult mental object but a pattern grounded in the person’s physical organization and linguistic training.

In “Mind and Verbal Dispositions,” Quine’s position becomes even more explicit: the mind can be treated as a system of dispositions to behavior, and dispositions themselves are physical traits or mechanisms. But that does not mean we can always specify the relevant physical mechanism in neural detail.

This yields one of the most important features of Quine’s philosophy of mind: mental predicates may pick out bodily states even when they do not do so in a neat, lawlike, reducible way. “Believes that snow is white,” “is anxious,” “expects rain,” or “understands French” may correspond to real bodily configurations, but the configurations may be heterogeneous, context-dependent, and practically impossible to specify in purely neurological terms.

So Quine is not saying: “mental terms are meaningless unless translated into physics.” He is saying: “if mental terms are legitimate, their truth must ultimately depend on facts about physical organisms and their relations to stimuli, language, and environment.”

8. Intentionality and propositional attitudes

The hardest part of Quine’s philosophy of mind is intentionality: belief, desire, fear, hope, expectation, meaning, reference, and the “aboutness” of thought. These are not merely sensations or bodily episodes; they seem to have objects and contents. I can believe that Caesar crossed the Rubicon, hope that my friend arrives safely, or fear that a storm is coming. Such states appear to relate a subject to a proposition.

Quine’s official response is famously severe. In “Word and Object,” he discusses Brentano’s thesis that intentional idioms are irreducible to the language of physical science. Quine accepts the irreducibility, but he draws the opposite conclusion from Brentano. Brentano sees irreducibility as vindicating the autonomy of the mental; Quine treats it as a reason to distrust intentional idioms in the austere language of science. They are indispensable in ordinary life, but they do not belong unchanged in canonical theory.

Quine especially distrusts propositional attitudes because they generate opaque contexts. In ordinary extensional logic, substitution of identicals preserves truth. But in belief contexts it often fails: Oedipus may believe that Jocasta is his wife without believing that his mother is his wife, even if Jocasta is his mother. Such contexts resist the extensional clarity Quine wants in canonical notation.

Spanish-language scholarship on Quine’s critique of propositional attitudes emphasizes this point: for Quine, the contents of mental states are inferred from observable behavior, but full reduction is impossible; indeterminacy of translation undermines the idea that speakers have sharply determinate meanings, thoughts, or beliefs privately present in the mind.

French scholarship adds another important nuance. Stéphane Lemaire’s French study of Quine’s extensionalism stresses that psychological phenomena, especially propositional attitudes, resist reduction to physical terms; the intentional phenomenon is something science cannot simply deny, but Quine’s extensionalist policy has difficulty incorporating it. The result is not a triumphant elimination of the mental but a tension between naturalistic ontology and the stubbornness of intentional discourse.

That is the right way to read Quine. He is not unaware that belief-talk is useful. He is painfully aware that it is useful. His problem is that usefulness in ordinary discourse does not automatically make an idiom fit for canonical ontology.

9. The two-level structure: canonical austerity and vernacular indispensability

The most charitable reconstruction of Quine’s philosophy of mind must distinguish two levels.

At the canonical level, Quine wants a regimented, extensional, scientifically disciplined language. At this level, the world consists of physical objects and sets. Organisms have physical states. Their behavior, neural organization, and environmental relations are legitimate objects of study. There are no private meanings, propositions, Cartesian egos, sense-data, or souls as additional entities.

At the vernacular and practical level, we continue to use mentalistic language. We attribute beliefs, desires, perceptions, intentions, hopes, fears, pains, and understandings. We do this because such language is efficient, socially embedded, and often indispensable. Verhaegh notes that Quine’s later work treats some mentalistic idioms as theoretically useful and practically indispensable; Quine’s position is therefore non-reductive in an important sense, even though his ontology remains physicalist.

This is why Quine is not a simple eliminativist. He does not recommend that we stop saying “Tom believes it will rain” or “Mary is in pain.” He recommends that philosophers not mistake the grammar of those sentences for a final inventory of reality. The mental idiom is useful, but its usefulness does not establish mental substances.

This two-level view also explains why Quine can be both a physicalist and a defender of mental vocabulary’s practical legitimacy. Mental predicates may classify physical states in ways that are too coarse, context-sensitive, or socially embedded to reduce neatly to neuroscience. In this respect, Quine’s view is close to Davidsonian anomalous monism or token physicalism with type pluralism: every particular mental event is physical, but mental kinds need not reduce cleanly to physical kinds. Verhaegh explicitly interprets Quine as a non-reductive physicalist of this sort.

10. Sense-data, private experience, and the anti-Cartesian turn

Quine’s rejection of sense-data is one of the deepest anti-Cartesian aspects of his philosophy of mind. Earlier empiricism often began from private sensory givens: impressions, ideas, experiences, or sense-data. The world was then constructed out of them. Quine rejects this as epistemologically and scientifically backward.

In “Word and Object,” he argues that physical things are known through sensory stimulation, but public objects are epistemologically and linguistically primary in language learning. Subjective sense qualities are derivative, not foundational.

This is a major departure from classical empiricism. Quine remains an empiricist in that sensory stimulation is the checkpoint of theory. But he is not an empiricist of private mental furniture. Experience is not a set of inner objects from which the world is built. Rather, experience is the causal interface between organism and world, and our theory of that interface is itself part of natural science.

This is why Quine’s naturalized epistemology is so important for philosophy of mind. The “given” is no longer a private object of consciousness. The human subject is a physical organism whose sensory receptors are stimulated and whose linguistic and theoretical behavior develops in response. The mind is not a theater in which private objects appear; it is an embodied system of dispositions, responses, and theory-mediated capacities.

11. Holism and the mental

Quine’s holism also shapes his philosophy of mind. In “Two Dogmas of Empiricism,” he argues that statements face experience not one by one but as part of a corporate body of theory. Our web of belief meets experience at its edges, and revisions can occur in different places depending on pragmatic and theoretical considerations.

This has a major implication for mental vocabulary. A mental term does not need to be justified by a one-to-one reduction to behavior. It may be justified by its role in a broader theory of persons, language, action, learning, and social interaction. That is why Quine can reject logical behaviorism while still requiring behavioral anchoring.

It also means that theological or spiritual vocabulary cannot be dismissed merely because it is not tied to a single observation sentence. Quine’s own holism allows theoretical posits to go far beyond immediate observation. The real question is whether those posits improve the total theory: whether they simplify, unify, predict, explain, and integrate with experience better than rivals.

That is why Quine’s critique of soul and spirit is not shallow verificationism. He is not saying every meaningful term must be directly observable. He is saying that ontology is governed by total theory, and total theory must ultimately answer to experience, simplicity, coherence, and scientific usefulness.

12. Theology: gods, souls, and ontological austerity

Quine’s most famous theological-sounding passage occurs in “Two Dogmas,” where he compares physical objects with Homeric gods as theoretical posits. His point is not that tables and gods are equally real. He explicitly accepts physical objects and rejects Homeric gods. The point is epistemological: both are posits within a conceptual scheme, but physical objects have proven vastly more useful for organizing experience.

This passage is often misunderstood. It is not relativism. It is not covert theology. It is a pragmatic naturalist account of ontology. We believe in the entities our best theory needs. Physical objects earn their place. Electrons, molecules, and sets may earn their place. Homeric gods do not.

A Catholic discussion of Quine’s ontology makes this point clearly: Quine’s rejection of gods is pragmatic rather than a simple dogmatic exclusion; he accepts entities such as particles and sets because they are indispensable to our best theories, while mythological gods are not.

The same applies to the soul. A soul, understood as an immaterial substance that bears consciousness, survives bodily death, and perhaps interacts with the body, would need to earn a place in the total theory. Quine’s own view is that mental life is already accommodated by physical organisms and their states. Thus the soul does not earn its keep.

But there is a theological complication. If God or the soul is treated as just another entity within a single mind-independent reality, then theological transcendence may be compromised. Islamic philosophical scholarship on Quine has developed this issue in a particularly interesting way. Muhammad Ahsan argues that importing Quinean ontological realism into analytic theology can conflict with Islamic conceptions of God as transcendent, ineffable, and not simply one entity among others. On that reading, Quine’s naturalistic ontology presses theology toward treating God as part of the same ontological inventory as ordinary things, which may be unacceptable for traditions emphasizing divine transcendence.

This is highly relevant to the soul. A Quinean ontology asks, “What entities must our theory quantify over?” But many theological traditions do not treat God, spirit, or soul as ordinary theoretical entities. Apophatic theology, for example, may resist the very demand that God be entered into a canonical ontology as one more value of a variable. A Quinean may reply that such resistance removes theology from disciplined ontology. The theologian may answer that Quine’s ontology is already too immanent, too naturalistic, and too tied to the grammar of scientific theory to handle transcendence.

So Quine gives theology a choice. Either theological entities enter the tribunal of total theory, in which case they must compete with other posits by Quinean standards; or they refuse that tribunal, in which case they cease to be ontology in Quine’s sense. This does not settle theology from within theology, but it does clarify the point of conflict.

13. The Quinean “soul”: what remains?

Can one extract any positive concept of soul from Quine? Only if “soul” is naturalized.

A Quinean “soul” would not be an immaterial substance. It would not be a private Cartesian ego. It would not be a detachable bearer of consciousness. It would not be an entity known by introspective certainty. Instead, it would be something like the whole embodied pattern of a person’s dispositions, speech, memory, temperament, responsiveness, affective tendencies, social capacities, and biological organization.

But Quine himself would probably resist calling this a soul, because the word carries ontological and theological baggage. He would prefer to speak of the organism, the person, the body, the nervous system, behavioral dispositions, and linguistic capacities.

Still, this naturalized “soul” concept may be useful for interpretation. It shows that Quine need not reduce persons to momentary behaviors or to bare particles. A person is a temporally extended physical object with a complex organization. In Quine’s physical ontology, even a human being can be treated as a four-dimensional physical object, with mental states located in temporal parts or stages of that object.

Thus Quine does not abolish personhood. He de-substantializes it. The person remains; the Cartesian soul does not.

14. Other minds

Quine’s approach to other minds follows naturally from his public theory of language and behavior. We do not infer other minds by first knowing our own private mental essence and then analogically projecting it onto bodies. We learn mental language socially, through the behavior of others and ourselves. The criteria for applying mental predicates are public from the beginning.

This does not mean that other minds are reducible to behavior. Rather, the evidence for other minds is behavioral, linguistic, and physiological. The reality underlying the attribution is bodily and dispositional. If I say someone is in pain, the statement may be true because of a physical state of that organism, even if my evidence is behavior and context.

This is a subtle alternative to both Cartesian skepticism and simple behaviorism. Quine does not try to refute skepticism about other minds from a first-person foundation. He dissolves the Cartesian setup by treating the very language of mind as public, socially acquired, and embedded in natural science.

15. Selfhood and the “I”

Quine has no rich doctrine of the self as a transcendental ego. The self is not an irreducible metaphysical subject standing behind experience. It is the person, the organism, the speaker, the body situated in a world and in a linguistic community.

This follows from his treatment of ontology. If the official domain contains physical objects and sets, and if persons are physical objects, then the self is not an additional entity over and above the person. There is no need to posit an inner owner of mental states. A pain is a state of the organism. A belief attribution is an attribution to a person. A memory is a capacity or state of a person. A conscious episode is an episode in the life of a body.

Theologically, this is one of the deepest clashes between Quine and soul doctrines. Many religious anthropologies distinguish sharply between body and soul, or between empirical person and spiritual subject. Quine’s framework flattens that distinction. The enduring person is a temporally extended physical being, not an immaterial subject housed in a body.

16. Quine and eliminative materialism

Quine should not be classified straightforwardly as an eliminative materialist. He does not say that belief, desire, pain, or consciousness are simply fictional in the way phlogiston is fictional. He continues to use and analyze mental vocabulary. He recognizes its practical indispensability.

But he is eliminativist with respect to certain philosophical reifications: meanings as entities, propositions as objects of belief, private sense-data as foundations, mental substances as souls, and perhaps sharply individuated intentional contents. These are not needed in canonical ontology.

So the best label is not eliminative materialism but austere, non-reductive physicalism with an eliminativist attitude toward mental objects. Quine eliminates the entities, not necessarily the idioms. Or more exactly: he eliminates the entities from canonical ontology while allowing many idioms to survive in ordinary, psychological, and social-scientific practice.

17. Quine and behaviorism

Quine is often called a behaviorist, and there is truth in that label if it is understood methodologically. He insists that language learning and evidence for mental attribution are publicly observable. He distrusts appeals to private meanings. He grounds semantics in stimulus, assent, dissent, and disposition.

But he is not a reductive logical behaviorist. He does not think every mental statement can be translated into a finite set of behavioral conditionals. Verhaegh’s reconstruction is especially clear on this: Quine rejects extreme behaviorism, allows mentalistic terms when they help systematize theory, and treats mental states as bodily states that may be specifiable only through mental vocabulary.

The right formula is: public evidence, physical ontology, non-reductive mental classification.

This also explains why Quine’s philosophy of mind remains relevant after the decline of mid-century behaviorism. His core claim is not that only behavior exists. It is that the scientific study of mind must begin with publicly available data and must not posit private entities without theoretical need. That claim survives even if one accepts cognitive science, neuroscience, computational modeling, and sophisticated psychology.

18. Quine and cognitive science

Quine’s framework is hospitable to cognitive science, provided cognitive science remains naturalistic. He would have no objection in principle to positing internal states, neural mechanisms, computational structures, or representational systems if they improve empirical theory. What he resists is the uncritical reification of meanings, propositions, and intentional objects without clear identity conditions.

This means Quine’s behaviorism is not anti-neuroscience. It is a discipline on evidence and ontology. He begins with public behavior because that is where language learning and interpretation are anchored, but he allows hidden physical mechanisms when science has reason to posit them.

A Quinean cognitive science would therefore be empirically robust, biologically grounded, suspicious of private introspective foundations, cautious about intentional content, and open to theoretical posits that earn their explanatory role.

19. The extracted Quinean philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Quinean philosophy of mind can be stated as the following set of theses.

First, mind is natural. There is no autonomous mental realm prior to science. Mind belongs to the same world as bodies, language, stimuli, and behavior.

Second, mental life is embodied. Pains, perceptions, beliefs, anxieties, and conscious episodes are states or episodes of physical organisms, even when our best descriptions of them are mental rather than neurological.

Third, the soul as immaterial substance is not part of canonical ontology. It explains no more than the organism and its states already explain, unless one independently posits disembodied spirits; Quine sees no theoretical pressure to do so.

Fourth, consciousness is real but not foundational. It is not a private epistemic given from which the world is constructed. It is a natural phenomenon of organisms, difficult to describe scientifically but not ontologically separate from the body.

Fifth, mental language is public in its acquisition. Words for sensations, emotions, perceptions, and attitudes are learned through public circumstances, symptoms, training, and correction.

Sixth, behavior is evidentially primary but not ontologically exhaustive. Quine is not saying that only behavior exists. He is saying that evidence for mental attribution and linguistic meaning must be publicly anchored.

Seventh, dispositions are central. Mental states can often be understood as bodily dispositions, capacities, or configurations manifested in speech, action, perception, and response.

Eighth, intentionality is the major unresolved pressure point. Belief, desire, and other propositional attitudes resist extensional regimentation. Quine treats them as practically indispensable but theoretically suspect in canonical science.

Ninth, there are no meanings or propositions as private mental objects. Meaning is not an inner museum exhibit; it is a function of use, stimulus, assent, translation, and theory.

Tenth, ontology is pragmatic but not arbitrary. Quine does not reject souls or gods by linguistic fiat. He asks whether they are indispensable to our best total theory. His answer is no.

Eleventh, theology is not dismissed as a mere distraction. It is forced into a difficult choice: either enter Quinean ontology as theory and face naturalistic standards, or reject those standards as inadequate to transcendence.

Twelfth, the self is the person, not a hidden ego. A human being is a temporally extended organism whose mental life is part of its physical life.

20. Strengths of Quine’s position

Quine’s approach has several major strengths.

It avoids Cartesian isolation. The mind is not trapped behind a veil of private ideas. Language, meaning, and mental attribution are social from the start.

It avoids crude reductionism. Quine does not require every mental predicate to be translated into physics. Mental vocabulary may remain useful because it classifies bodily states in ways important to human life and social explanation.

It avoids ontological inflation. Quine refuses to multiply entities merely because grammar invites reification. “Believes that p” does not automatically require propositions as objects; “has an experience” does not automatically require private experience-objects; “has a soul” does not automatically require an immaterial substance.

It integrates philosophy of mind with epistemology, language, and science. Mind is not a separate metaphysical department. It is part of the same network as learning, evidence, theory, and ontology.

It gives theology a serious challenge rather than an easy dismissal. Quine’s question is not “Can I mock religious language?” but “What ontological commitments does the total theory really require?” That is a stronger and more philosophically important challenge.

21. Weaknesses and unresolved problems

Quine’s position also has real limitations.

First, his treatment of consciousness is thin. Saying that consciousness is a bodily or nervous phenomenon may be true, but it does not explain subjective character. Quine gives us a naturalistic discipline, not a phenomenology.

Second, intentionality remains unstable. Quine admits the practical indispensability of belief and desire language while denying it full canonical status. But if mature psychology, linguistics, cognitive science, and social science cannot avoid intentional idioms, then the boundary between “serious science” and “useful vernacular” becomes harder to maintain.

Third, the exclusion of propositions and meanings may be too austere. Many philosophers argue that semantic and cognitive theory require structured contents, even if not private Cartesian entities. Quine’s indeterminacy arguments make such contents suspect, but they do not make the need for semantic theory disappear.

Fourth, his theology-facing ontology may beg the question against transcendence. If God or soul is understood as supernatural, ineffable, or not an entity among entities, then Quine’s demand for canonical ontological commitment may already have translated theology into an alien register. The Islamic critique noted above is especially forceful here: a Quinean framework may distort traditions for which God is not merely a member of the inventory of beings.

Fifth, Quine’s view of the person is deliberately impersonal. It explains how to discipline talk of mind, but it does not capture the existential, moral, liturgical, or first-person significance that “soul” often carries in religious and humanistic contexts.

22. Final synthesis

Quine’s philosophy of mind is an embodied, naturalistic, public, and ontologically austere account of mental life. It rejects the soul as a separate substance, rejects meanings and propositions as private mental entities, rejects sense-data as epistemic foundations, and rejects Cartesian first philosophy. It accepts consciousness, pain, belief-talk, and ordinary mental vocabulary as real features of our life and language, but it refuses to infer from that vocabulary a separate mental realm.

The most precise formula is this: for Quine, mental states are states of physical organisms, described by mental predicates whose evidence is public and whose utility may be indispensable, but whose grammar must not be mistaken for ontology.

His position is therefore neither simple behaviorism nor simple eliminativism. It is a non-reductive physicalism disciplined by behaviorism, holism, and extensional regimentation. It allows mental language to remain useful while denying mental substances a place in the final inventory of reality.

Theologically, Quine’s framework is severe but not trivial. It does not refute God or the soul by definition. It subjects them to the same naturalistic standards as other posits. A disembodied soul, an immortal spirit, or a transcendent divine mind would need to earn ontological commitment within the total theory, and Quine sees no such need. But traditions that understand God or soul as transcending the ordinary inventory of entities may reject Quine’s tribunal itself. That is the deepest point of conflict: not merely whether souls exist, but whether Quine’s naturalized ontology is the right court before which soul, spirit, and God must appear.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
